tone_and_voice: Reference-establishing, not evaluative. Baseline writing must resist the temptation to draw conclusions about programme effectiveness -- its entire purpose is documenting Time Zero, not judging performance. Language should be neutral and descriptive of the starting condition, never celebratory or critical of a programme that has not yet been implemented.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must explicitly state this is a Time Zero reference point and that findings should not be read as evaluating any intervention -- this framing must appear in the first paragraph, not buried in a methodology section.
section_rules.discussion: Any respondent-reported sense of recent trend or change (e.g. 'things have improved lately') must be explicitly flagged as PRE-programme context, not evidence of this programme's effect, since no intervention has yet been evaluated under this Results Framework at baseline.
section_rules.methodology: Must state the exact question wording that will be repeated at midline/endline, since baseline value is largely a function of enabling exact future comparability.
section_rules.limitations: State explicitly that no causal or comparative claims can be made at baseline -- this is a structural feature of baseline reporting, not a limitation to apologize for.
section_rules.recommendations: Baseline recommendations should be about PROGRAMME DESIGN (e.g. 'the ToC should explicitly target X, given its dominance in baseline data') and about MEASUREMENT DESIGN (e.g. 'lock these questions for midline/endline'), not about implementation actions -- there is no implementation to act on yet.
section_rules.policy_implications: Rare and should be framed as 'this baseline suggests the programme's policy-relevant focus should be X', never as a standalone policy conclusion independent of the programme design process.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Should focus on methodological lessons for maintaining baseline-to-endline comparability, since this is the single most important baseline-stage discipline.
section_rules.conclusion: Must restate that these are the official Time Zero reference values, and name what they will be compared against (midline, endline) and when.
section_rules.annexes: Include the complete, exact question set used, flagged explicitly as 'to be repeated verbatim at future rounds' -- this is the single most operationally important annex content for a baseline.
section_rules.evidence_style: Present findings as clean reference values (e.g. 'affordability: 8 mentions, the highest of any theme') without interpretive framing about whether this is good or bad -- that judgment belongs to midline/endline, not baseline.

common_kpis: dominant theme ranking (to establish programme design priority)
demographic/regional composition (sampling reference for future rounds)
common_risks: baseline being misread as an evaluation of a not-yet-implemented programme
future rounds using different question wording, breaking comparability
conflating respondent-perceived recent trend with programme-attributable change
typical_recommendations: formal adoption of baseline values as official Results Framework Time Zero
explicit Theory of Change targeting of the baseline's dominant concern
identical question-wording and sampling framework locked for midline/endline
typical_policy_implications: programme design and Theory of Change targeting decisions (design-stage, not implementation-stage)
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Donors expect baselines to be methodologically rigorous and explicitly framed as non-evaluative reference points -- a baseline that reads like a performance evaluation of the pre-programme condition raises credibility concerns about the whole Results Framework.

forbidden_behaviors: Never draw a conclusion about programme effectiveness at baseline -- no intervention has yet been evaluated under this Results Framework.
Never attribute a respondent-reported recent trend to a programme that has not yet been implemented or evaluated.
Never present baseline findings with celebratory or critical framing -- baseline is a neutral reference point, not a performance judgment.
